text.title: Browser Automation Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every browser-automation artefact must obey. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Automation MUST use assertion-based explicit waits (waitForSelector / expect.poll / etc.). `sleep`, `setTimeout`, or arbitrary `waitForTimeout` calls are forbidden.

rationale.source: Playwright official docs + Internal — flake audit

rule.rationale: Time-based waits race against page state; assertions on state are inherently flake-resistant.

rule.statement: Selector strategy MUST follow priority: data-test-id > role > text > CSS. Each interaction documents which level its selector uses.
rule.rationale: Lower-priority selectors break on CSS refactors; higher-priority ones survive design changes.

rule.statement: Per-screen Page Object MUST be the only module touching the DOM; tests assert against domain methods, not selectors directly.

rationale.source: Martin Fowler — Page Object pattern + Selenium documentation

rule.rationale: Selector churn rippled across tests is the most common flake source; POM concentrates the change to one file.

rule.statement: Suite flake_rate (failed-then-passed-on-retry / total runs) MUST stay <= 2% per 100 runs. Above the budget, suite goes red.

rationale.source: Google testing-on-the-toilet guidance + Internal — flake audit

rule.rationale: Without a budget, flakes accumulate; engineers learn to retry instead of fix.

rule.statement: Data extraction primitives MUST be separated from interaction primitives. One file scrapes, one file clicks; mixing them produces tests that fail for both reasons.
rule.rationale: When a test fails, you need to know whether the selector broke or the data shape changed; mixed code obscures the signal.
